title: Indonesian Beef Rendang (Pressure Cooker)

ingredients.name: beef (brisket or chuck), cut into 4 cm cubes

subs.name: goat meat (mutton) cut into large pieces — increase pressure to 5 whistles
200 ml coconut milk powder dissolved in 400 ml water

ingredients.name: desiccated or freshly grated coconut, dry-roasted until golden
visual_checks: this is kerisik — dry-roast on a tawa for 4 minutes until deep golden
ingredients.notes: this is kerisik — dry-roast on a tawa for 4 minutes until deep golden

ingredients.name: fresh ginger (substitute for galangal)
visual_checks: galangal is traditional; ginger gives a similar warmth
ingredients.notes: galangal is traditional; ginger gives a similar warmth

subs.name: 1 tsp lemon zest added with the spice paste

ingredients.name: kaffir lime leaves
visual_checks: available at Indian specialty stores; substitute with lime zest
ingredients.notes: available at Indian specialty stores; substitute with lime zest

subs.name: zest of 1 lime or 3–4 curry leaves for a gentle citrus note

ingredients.name: red chilli powder (Kashmiri for colour, bird's eye for heat)

steps.text: Dry-roast the grated coconut in a tawa on medium heat, stirring constantly, for 3–4 minutes until dark golden. Grind to a coarse paste with a little water — this is the kerisik that gives rendang its signature dry texture. Set aside.
Blend onion, garlic, ginger and red chilli powder with 3 tbsp water into a smooth spice paste.

Heat oil in the pressure cooker pot on medium-high flame. Add cinnamon, cloves and star anise; fry for 30 seconds until fragrant. Add the spice paste and sauté, stirring, for 6–8 minutes until the oil separates from the paste and the raw smell is gone.
Add the beef pieces and turmeric; sear, turning, for 4–5 minutes until lightly browned on all sides.

Add coconut milk, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, jaggery and salt. Stir well. Seal the pressure cooker and cook on high pressure for 4 whistles, then reduce to low and cook for 10 more minutes. Allow pressure to release naturally — about 10 minutes.

Open the cooker — the beef should be fork-tender. Add the kerisik paste and stir through. Cook uncovered on medium heat, stirring every few minutes, until the gravy reduces completely and the beef starts to fry in the thick coconut oil — about 20–25 minutes. The rendang is ready when the colour deepens to dark brown and no liquid remains.
Taste for salt. Remove whole spices and lemongrass before serving. Rendang tastes significantly better the next day — the spices deepen overnight.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: beef → goat meat (mutton) cut into large pieces — increase pressure to 5 whistles (flavour profile is very similar); coconut-milk → 200 ml coconut milk powder dissolved in 400 ml water (widely available and shelf-stable); kaffir-lime-leaves → zest of 1 lime or 3–4 curry leaves for a gentle citrus note; lemongrass → 1 tsp lemon zest added with the spice paste
indian_kitchen_adaptation: The pressure cooker adaptation here is designed for Indian kitchens. Use brisket or any bone-in beef available at your local butcher. Replace galangal (rare in India) with fresh ginger. Kaffir lime leaves are increasingly available at specialty grocers; failing that, a strip of lime zest stirred in at the end delivers a similar citrus lift. Desiccated coconut (dry khopra) works for the kerisik. Jaggery replaces palm sugar.
